episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Indigenous Lands & Innovation
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down Preventing AI extractivism: the case for braiding indigenous data justice with ABS for stronger AI data governance and TMU students to safeguard Residential School Survivor testimonies, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What infrastructure, governance, and safeguards must be in place before Indigenous communities build digital archives to ensure long-term functionality, community control, and protection from data extraction? Topics include: Format obsolescence and technical sovereignty in digital preservation, Shared infrastructure governance models versus isolated community systems, AI extractivism and machine-readable metadata protection (TK Labels), Temporal asymmetry between knowledge preservation and data extraction, Regional Indigenous consortium potential for shared technical capacity in the Cariboo.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Denisiqi Services Society

Digital Archives as Memory Keepers
articles.summary: Digital Archives as Memory Keepers

Statutory Holidays at Animikii

articles.title: Niiwin Demo
articles.summary: Digital Archives as Memory Keepers

discussion.central_question: What infrastructure, governance, and safeguards must be in place before Indigenous communities build digital archives to ensure long-term functionality, community control, and protection from data extraction?
discussion.riley_position: Digital archives require shared infrastructure models (like Niiwin), machine-readable governance metadata (TK Labels), and community representation in platform governance to balance dependency risks with sustainability.
riley_key_evidence: Format migration and open standards are sovereignty questions, not just technical preferences
Local Contexts TK Labels are machine-readable metadata that can legally restrict AI training data scraping
Shared platform governance through co-operative models reduces per-community maintenance burden while preserving control
discussion.casey_position: Archives are literal infrastructure requiring years of relationship-building and governance protocols, but face a dangerous temporal asymmetry where knowledge takes years to build trust but minutes to extract—requiring succession planning and careful sequencing before going online.
casey_key_evidence: TMU residential school testimony project illustrates how digitization is easy but 50-year preservation is the real challenge
University digital library projects have failed, causing materials to disappear into institutional limbo
A language recording requiring three years of relationship-building can be scraped for AI training in seconds
discussion.resolution: Both speakers converged on the necessity of community data governance policies, TK Labels, written succession plans, and strategic partnerships before launch. Riley acknowledged the risk of moving too fast; Casey agreed that tools exist and waiting creates its own risk of knowledge-holder loss. They agreed the tension between careful governance and urgency cannot be cleanly resolved but can be managed through proper sequencing.
topics_covered: Format obsolescence and technical sovereignty in digital preservation
Shared infrastructure governance models versus isolated community systems
AI extractivism and machine-readable metadata protection (TK Labels)
Temporal asymmetry between knowledge preservation and data extraction
Regional Indigenous consortium potential for shared technical capacity in the Cariboo
